canonical_definition: Across the provided literature, MaliciousInstruct is consistently characterized as a benchmark dataset composed of "malicious" or "harmful" instructions. Its primary purpose is to evaluate the safety of large language models, with papers using it to measure the construct of Safety. Specifically, it is employed to test a model's "safety defenses" and its "resistance... to harmful queries." A recurring theme is its application as an out-of-domain (OOD) or out-of-distribution test set, with one paper using it to "assess transferability" of defenses. One source specifies that the benchmark contains "100 broader harmful instructions." In evaluations, it is often used alongside other harmful benchmarks such as AdvBench and HEx-PHI.
papers: AfriMed-QA: A Pan-African, Multi-Specialty, Medical Question-Answering Benchmark Dataset
Answering Complex Geographic Questions by Adaptive Reasoning with Visual Context and External Commonsense Knowledge
EQA-RM: A Generative Embodied Reward Model with Test-time Scaling
Legal Fact Prediction: The Missing Piece in Legal Judgment Prediction
RED: Unleashing Token-Level Rewards from Holistic Feedback via Reward Redistribution
Forewarned is Forearmed:  Harnessing LLMs for Data Synthesis via Failure-induced Exploration
Probe before You Talk: Towards Black-box Defense against Backdoor Unalignment for Large Language Models
Safety Alignment Should be Made More Than Just a Few Tokens Deep
Understanding and Enhancing the Transferability of Jailbreaking Attacks

definitions.text: A dataset of malicious instructions used as an out-of-domain test set to evaluate a model's safety defenses.
